ontology.foodon_gap_note: No specific FoodOn entry for cheese-topped Middle Eastern flatbread; mapped to generic bread food product as closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and similar baked products; no specific code for cheese-topped ekmek exists; this is the closest defensible category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for cheese-topped Middle Eastern bread; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of cheese ekmek weighs approximately 150 g, providing around 420 kcal, 18 g protein, 18 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate per serving.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bread, cheese, SR Legacy FDC ID 172023; blended with plain white bread SR Legacy FDC ID 269714 and part-skim mozzarella SR Legacy FDC ID 170845
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for cheese ekmek exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cheese bread (FDC ID 172023), plain white bread (FDC ID 269714), and part-skim mozzarella (FDC ID 170845), blended in approximate proportions (70% bread base, 30% mozzarella topping) to reflect the described dish composition. Input values from the GrabFood record (confidence 0.7, ai_generated provenance) were cross-checked against this USDA-derived estimate and found broadly consistent; USDA SR Legacy is used as the sole primary evidence basis. Calcium 200 mg/100g is consistent with mozzarella contribution per USDA SR Legacy. Sodium 433 mg/100g is consistent with salted dough plus mozzarella per USDA SR Legacy. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a secondary aggregator and was not used as primary nutrient evidence.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 31(12):2281-2283
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for cheese ekmek in any approved source. GI estimated at 70 based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for white bread (GI ~70–75, glucose reference scale) as the primary carbohydrate vehicle, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283), an approved peer-reviewed source. The mozzarella cheese topping contributes protein and fat which partially attenuate postprandial glycaemic response; however, the refined wheat flour bread base dominates the GI. GI 70 is used as a conservative analogous estimate. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (70 × 45.0) / 100 = 31.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 433 mg per 100 g exceeds the 400 mg/100 g threshold; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 650 mg sodium, representing 28–43% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 1500–2000 mg for hypertensive individuals.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 60–70 g per occasion, request reduced salt in the dough and reduced cheese quantity, and avoid pairing with other high-sodium foods such as processed meats, pickles, or salty sauces in the same meal.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 30 g/100 g from refined wheat flour with GI ~70 (high) and low dietary fibre 1.67 g/100 g; a 150 g serving delivers 45 g carbohydrate and a glycemic load of 31.5, which is clinically high and will produce a significant postprandial blood glucose rise.
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portion to one small piece (60–70 g), pair with a protein or non-starchy vegetable to blunt glycaemic response, request wholemeal flour substitution to lower GI and increase fibre, and monitor postprandial blood glucose after consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g exceeds the 5 g/100 g CVD threshold, sodium 433 mg/100 g is elevated, trans fat 0.33 g/100 g and cholesterol 30 mg/100 g are present; a 150 g serving delivers approximately 10 g saturated fat and 0.5 g trans fat, both of which raise LDL cholesterol and increase cardiovascular risk.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat cheese ekmek as an occasional food, limit portion to 60–70 g, request reduced cheese and salt, and avoid pairing with other saturated-fat-rich or high-sodium foods to keep total meal saturated fat below 7 g and sodium below 600 mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.67 g/100 g exceeds the RA pro-inflammatory threshold of 5 g/100 g; sodium 433 mg/100 g also exceeds the RA sodium threshold; both nutrients promote TNF-alpha and IL-6 inflammatory cytokine activity.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should minimise consumption, request reduced cheese and salt, and prefer wholemeal flour variants to increase anti-inflammatory fibre; olive oil in the recipe provides minor oleocanthal benefit but is insufficient to offset the saturated fat and sodium burden.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cheese Ekmek, also known as Cheese Bread or Peynirli Ekmek, is a baked bread product of Middle Eastern origin, popular in Singapore's shawarma and kebab food stalls. The dish consists of a soft, leavened wheat-flour dough base that is freshly baked and generously topped with melted shredded mozzarella cheese, producing a savoury, chewy, and comforting bread with a golden, slightly crispy cheese crust. The bread base is made from refined wheat flour, water, yeast, salt, and a small amount of olive oil, giving it a mild flavour and soft crumb. The mozzarella topping melts during baking to form a stretchy, lightly browned cheese layer. Per 100 g, cheese ekmek provides approximately 280 kcal, 12 g protein, 12 g fat (6.67 g saturated), 30 g carbohydrate, 1.67 g dietary fibre, and 433 mg sodium. The high sodium content (from both the salted dough and mozzarella) and elevated saturated fat make this food a concern for individuals with hypertension, cardiovascular disease, and rheumatoid arthritis. The refined carbohydrate base with a high glycemic index (~70) and low fibre content makes it unsuitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes unless portion is strictly controlled. A standard serving is one piece (approximately 150 g), delivering around 420 kcal and 45 g carbohydrate with a glycemic load of 31.5. Cheese ekmek is vegetarian and halal-suitable but contains gluten and dairy allergens. It is commonly consumed as a snack, light meal, or accompaniment to shawarma and kebab dishes in Singapore's Middle Eastern food outlets.

Is cheese bread high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for cheese ekmek exists in USDA FoodData Central or HPB Singapore database. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy entries for cheese bread (FDC ID 172023), plain white bread (FDC ID 269714), and part-skim mozzarella (FDC ID 170845), blended to reflect the described dish composition, and cross-validated against the GrabFood input values (confidence 0.7, ai_generated). The adapted USDA-derived values are broadly consistent with the input. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread analogue per Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium because the exact dish formulation (dough-to-cheese ratio, salt level) is not independently verified by a primary approved source.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this specific cheese bread preparation is not available in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy cheese bread entry (FDC ID 172023) as of 2026-04-17. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the adapted USDA SR Legacy source for this blended dish composition as of 2026-04-17. 2. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: cheese bread FDC ID 172023, plain white bread FDC ID 269714, and part-skim mozzarella FDC ID 170845, blended in approximate proportions (70% bread base, 30% mozzarella topping). Input GrabFood values (ai_generated, confidence 0.7) were cross-checked and found broadly consistent with the USDA-derived estimate. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy is the sole approved primary source used. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) is a secondary aggregator and was not used as primary nutrient evidence. 3. GI estimated at 70 from University of Sydney GI Database white bread analogue, consistent with Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283); no direct GI measurement for cheese ekmek exists in any approved source. GL per serving = (70 × 45.0) / 100 = 31.5. 4. trans_fat_g 0.33 g/100g is a trace value consistent with dairy fat content of mozzarella per USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 170845. 5. vitamin_c_mg 0.33 mg/100g is a trace value from minor ingredients, consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for bread products. 6. Ontology: FoodOn FOODON_03315595 (bread food product) and FoodEx2 A000E (Bread and similar products) are used as the closest defensible approximate matches; no exact ontology code exists for cheese-topped Middle Eastern flatbread. SNOMED CT left null — no specific code identified. 7. Multilingual names provided for Singaporean context (English, Chinese Simplified, Chinese Traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this dish is served in Singapore. Turkish romanized forms 'Peynirli Ekmek' and 'Ekmek Peynirli' included as the dish name derives from Turkish.

GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database white bread analogue per Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care, approved peer-reviewed journal). Multilingual names cover English, Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi per Singaporean dish rules. All three required health conditions (Hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, Cardiovascular Disease) are present with specific clinical guidance.
